query: effect of intensifying screens on contrast in extraoral radiographs

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiographs can show the extent of the infection and help evaluate areas that are not obvious to visual evaluation. These include intraoral periapical radiographs, bitewing radiographs, and panoramic radiographs. These radiographs help to identify the extent of dental caries, the presence of a periapical abscess, alveolar bone loss concerning periodontitis, and third molar eruptions. The dental practitioner’s physical examination must confirm all radiographic evidence for a full evaluation and accurate diagnosis. CT scans and MRIs help assess deep-space infections and osteomyelitis. Cone-beam CT is becoming increasingly common in clinical settings to better visualize areas of oral infections. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographs are an important diagnostic tool for diagnosing TDIs and serve as a baseline when compared to follow-up appointments. Intra-oral radiographs may include periapical radiographs (size 0 sensor/film, paralleling technique) or occlusal radiographs (size 2 sensor/film). Conventional radiology may not be possible in young children due to compliance, and a clinician may consider alternative views, such as lateral obliques. However, it is important to consider if taking an image and exposing the child to radiation will positively affect the diagnosis or treatment provided. Clinicians must work within the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principles to minimize radiation doses. The use of pulp testing in children is challenging; it is subjective and relies on the child’s understanding and cooperation. [19] False-positive/negative results can be misleading in clinical situations and therefore are not recommended in the IADT guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A diagnosis of dental fluorosis is contingent on a positive fluoride history within the critical window of tooth development and a visual intraoral examination. [21] The utilization of effective lighting and a dental mirror is essential. Magnification can be a useful adjunct, especially in subtle cases of dental fluorosis. In cases where the surface enamel is pitted, a dental probe can be used carefully so as not to damage the already compromised tooth structure further. Radiographs and other dental investigations, such as sensibility testing, are of limited use where dental fluorosis is concerned. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Despite its numerous advantages, it is essential to acknowledge the limitations of cone beam computed tomography. For instance, it may have lower contrast and higher radiation dosages when compared to traditional radiographic techniques such as intraoral and panoramic radiography. Therefore, cone beam computed tomography should be restricted to situations where its benefits outweigh the potential risks. Its usage should be limited to cases where it is justifiable. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of dental caries involves using various techniques, such as the visual-tactile method, radiographs, fiberoptic illumination (FOTI), and digital fiberoptic illumination (DIFOTI). The most common and easiest method is the conventional visual-tactile method. Several radiographic techniques are effective for detecting caries radiographically, such as intraoral periapical radiographs and bitewing radiographs for occlusion-proximal caries.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Traumatic Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Both intraoral and extraoral radiographs, in addition to CT scans, can help identify dental and periodontal fractures. The recommended radiographs are 1 parallel periapical view, 2 additional views with a vertical and horizontal angle, and an occlusal view. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Skull Imaging -- Computed Tomography
retrieved_docs.content: The skull imaging study has a long list of indications that can practically be classified as trauma, benign or malignant focal skull lesions, and systemic pathologies affecting the skull (see Video. Radiologic Imaging Study. Large Skull Base Mass). CT is the principal modality in the imaging of traumatic skull lesions since it not only provides perfect visualization of cortical bone but also offers a very short imaging time, allowing immediate intervention before brain damage or death. Besides, it is the most accurate method of detecting a cerebrospinal fluid leak. Multidetector computed tomography (MDCT) allows rapid volumetric image data acquisition, and with the use of sub-millimetric slice thickness, it permits multiplanar reformations and 3-dimensional (3D) reconstructions. [8] [11] [12] 2D axial and coronal images are more sensitive and reliable than 3D reconstructions in the detection and detailed evaluation of skull fractures. However, since 3D imaging provides a global view of the fracture pattern and significantly contributes to preoperative planning, it is widely requested by surgeons. For this reason, 3D reconstructions should be added to the imaging study in skull fracture cases, especially in those detected in the maxillofacial area. [8] CT and MRI are complementary modalities for imaging focal skull lesions, and both are performed in most cases, especially in skull base lesions. CT is the ideal method for evaluating the inner and outer tables of cortical bone, detecting bone lysis or sclerosis, precise localization of the lesion, and visualizing the mineralized part(s) of the lesion. [3] [4] [5] [6] [13] [14] [15] [16] Currently, non-contrast CT of the whole skull without further contrast-enhanced CT examination is recommended in cases where MRI is included in the imaging study. In limiting exposure to ionizing radiation, contrast-enhanced CT examination is indicated only in the case of contraindications to MRI [15] . Certain CT findings guide the diagnosis and significantly shorten the differential diagnosis list in evaluating skull lesions. For example, while a regular cortical expansion and remodeling of the bone with smooth thinning suggest benign pathologies that exhibit a slow-growing pattern, permeative and destructive changes strongly support the presence of a rapidly progressing process such as aggressive inflammation or malignity. [16] Pathologies such as fibrous dysplasia, hemangiomas, and Paget disease offer highly specific CT findings. Fibrous dysplasia characteristically appears as an intradiploic expansile mass showing a ground-glass matrix on CT, while in most cases, it appears misleadingly aggressive on MRI. Hemangiomas are seen on CT as well-circumscribed intradiploic osteolytic lesions showing a sunburst pattern of coarse trabeculae with spicules or radiations. Thanks to this characteristic appearance, hemangiomas can be accurately diagnosed by CT without further MRI examination. Likewise, Paget disease also shows characteristic CT findings, including bone expansion, cortical thickening, and trabecular coarsening. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Digital Radiography - The computed radiographic image receptor, an elementary form of a digital detector, is similar yet different from screen-film imaging in a few ways. One similarity is their mutual use of X-ray-sensitive plates. Computed radiography using storage phosphors uses trapped electrons in metastable states to produce an image instead of a scintillator used in screen-film radiography. Metastable electrons are released with a stimulating laser beam, with the emission of light on the return of these electrons to a ground state. [11] This light emission is used to establish an image. Computed radiography also comparatively has a reduced radiation dose and better contrast resolution when compared to screen radiography (although the resolution is worse in digital radiography). Flat Panel Detectors are classically associated with digital detectors. It is faster than film radiography and includes direct and indirect systems. Explained simply, indirect systems require a latent image to be released as a light, then converted to a charge and a digital image. In contrast, direct systems directly convert x-rays into charge (and subsequently an image) without this intermediate step. Indirect systems employ a cesium iodide scintillator, which constitutes most flat-panel detectors, while direct systems employ amorphous selenium.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Visual-tactile examination of carious lesions is not sufficient for assessing interproximal or occlusal surfaces. Therefore, radiography is commonly used to assess and detect caries, as it gives the practitioner additional information about the clinical progression. When x-rays go through tooth structures, the beam becomes attenuated according to the different structures within its path. Dental hard tissues and bone appear radiopaque because of their calcified structure, which stops or absorbs the x-rays. Soft tissues are not as calcified as bone or teeth and will allow the x-rays to penetrate through them, making the structure appear radiolucent. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Various radiographic techniques can be used for caries detection, but posterior bitewing, periapical, and panoramic views are the most popular. Posterior bitewings show the occlusion of the upper and lower premolars and molars. They are the standard view for detecting dental caries and are considered the radiograph of choice for diagnosing interproximal caries. Traditionally, these images are the first radiographs obtained in patients with low caries risk. [8] Periapical views show the whole tooth, from crown to root, and can help detect anterior proximal caries, periodontal disease, and periapical lesions. Lastly, panoramic views give an overview of the whole maxilla and mandible. This method can serve as a quick way to visualize caries; however, it does not provide sufficient information to detect incipient caries. It is important to note that radiography alone is not an adequate method for caries detection because it cannot distinguish between cavitated and non-cavitated or active and arrested lesions. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiography is widely used as a diagnostic tool in daily dental practice. It is estimated that dentists are responsible for more than one-quarter of all medical radiographs in Europe. Since the discovery of X-rays 120 years ago, dental radiographs have been the primary source of diagnostic information for the oral and maxillofacial complex. However, their use is limited because 2D imaging techniques cannot display complex 3D anatomical structures and related pathologies. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Orthopedic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Image intensifier: The image intensifier is an important component that converts the intensity of the remnant ray beam into an image suitable for video capture or display on the monitor. Image intensifiers electronically amplify electron optics to obtain a higher quality image at modest dose radiation. In recent years, the image intensifier with video capture has been replaced by flat-panel receptors. These are physically compact, flexible, without image distortion, and the entrance dose is proportional to the field of view. Television and recording system: High-quality displays are required for the investigator to appreciate fine details and subtle contrast differences. Usually, flat-panel LCDs with high contrast ratios are calibrated to achieve the widest range of grey levels possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma -- Evaluation -- Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: Plain radiography is done for dental assessment and bony invasion, but it is of limited value.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging becomes necessary when deep-space infection, abscess formation, or unusual progression is suspected. A panoramic radiograph (orthopantomogram) helps identify periapical lesions, impacted teeth, or osseous involvement. Periapical radiographs allow for detailed assessment of individual teeth. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography imaging serves as the gold standard for evaluating deep-space involvement and distinguishing cellulitis from abscess. Ultrasound offers a noninvasive method for detecting superficial abscesses, especially in the submandibular or buccal regions.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There is active research into tools to supplement the diagnosis of fluorosis, such as the use of short-wavelength infrared light. Under this spectrum, dental fluorosis appears in high contrast, which can help identify more subtle cases of fluorosis. [23] This also enables dental professionals to stage the grade of fluorosis more accurately. However, the use of these tools is not yet commonplace in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Multi-cystic or Solid type appears as  multi-locular radiolucency that resembles honeycomb or soap bubble appearance. Expansion of the buccal and lingual cortices can be seen as well (Fig. 1 and 2) . At the same time, the uni-cystic type shows Well demarcated Uni-locular translucency that mimics dentigerous cysts. An impacted tooth may be present, and the peripheral type may show superficial erosion of the alveolar bone in few cases (Fig. 3) . Malignant type is usually similar to multicystic type. In these cases, the use of functional PET/ CT scans is extremely helpful in determining the extent of soft tissue involvement and distant metastases. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 853
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is classified as a Computed tomography imaging ‑ action and functions as a method of Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). This indicates that CT follow-up represents a specific imaging approach used when a standard listed CT procedure is not applicable, serving as a methodological option within broader diagnostic and interventional CT practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2519
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a basic imaging modality, and both computerized tomography of brain and chest and computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative or mapped imaging studies derived from the routine chest x-ray, indicating that these CT examinations may be employed for more detailed evaluation when the findings from a standard chest x-ray are insufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium is primarily mapped from the computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head. The computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head is clinically associated with خرف (dementia) and frequently co‑occurs with خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia), highlighting head CT imaging as a key diagnostic modality in the assessment of dementia, especially age‑related forms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is identified as a method_of, meaning it serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action, providing a structured approach for using CT scans in follow‑up assessments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Plain X-ray techniques encompass tomografie as a subordinate imaging method, indicating that tomografie is classified as a child technique within the broader category of plain X-ray imaging. This relationship highlights that tomografie derives from or is considered a specialized extension of standard plain X-ray procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium serves as the primary reference for the abbreviated term ct scan abdomen. The ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). Both imaging modalities are employed to evaluate abdominal pathology, and their clinical association reflects complementary roles in the diagnostic work‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium and computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium are both identified as imaging studies that are mapped from a routine chest x‑ray, indicating that these contrast‑enhanced CT examinations represent more extensive diagnostic extensions of the basic chest radiograph.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: as a child modality, indicating that tomography (including CT) is considered a specialized subcategory within the spectrum of plain radiographic techniques. This network of hierarchical, mapping, and metadata relationships integrates various CT examinations—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced, single‑region and multi‑region—under unified anatomical and procedural categories, supporting consistent terminology and facilitating comprehensive patient care planning.
